pattern_recognition: Dynamic Programming, as it involves breaking down the problem into smaller subproblems and solving them to build up the solution. Signals include overlapping subproblems and optimal substructure.

approaches.title: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Generate all possible subsequences of both strings and compare them to find the longest common one.
approaches.algorithm: Recursively explore all subsequences of both strings, keeping track of the maximum length found.

approaches.title: Optimal Approach
approaches.intuition: Use dynamic programming to store results of subproblems in a table to avoid redundant calculations.
approaches.algorithm: Create a 2D array where dp[i][j] represents the length of LCS of text1[0..i-1] and text2[0..j-1]. Fill the table based on character matches and previously computed values.

dry_run: For text1 = 'abcde' and text2 = 'ace', start with an empty dp table. Compare characters: 'a' matches 'a', so dp[1][1] = 1. 'b' does not match 'c', so dp[2][2] = max(dp[2][1], dp[1][2]). Continue filling the table until dp[5][3] gives the length of LCS, which is 3.
edge_cases: Both strings are empty, one string is empty, both strings are single characters, strings with all characters the same, strings of significantly different lengths, and very large strings leading to performance issues.
common_pitfalls: 1) Forgetting to initialize the dp array correctly can lead to incorrect results. 2) Not considering all characters in both strings can result in missed matches. 3) Failing to handle cases where one string is empty, which should return 0.
